sentences.text: My name is Ruth Arkiss.
A-R-K-I-S-S.
It was pronounced 'hoy-mun' in Germany, and in America, we call it 'hyoo-mun,' spelled H-E-U-M-A-N-N.
My birth date is February 19, 1930.
Today I am 66 years old.
I was born in Cologne, but I did not live there.
I lived in Leverkusen Wiesdorf, which is now just called Leverkusen.
It became one big city out of several small ones.
Leverkusen is L-E-V-E-R-K-U-S-E-N.
That is in Germany.
The community I grew up in seemed friendly.
We, as a Jewish family-- I had my identification as a Jewish child, and a very Jewish home.
We celebrated all the holy days and Friday evening Shabbat.
Later on I took Hebrew lessons.
And I-- my mother-- my mother was a widow.
We lived in that house, which my father built, all my life in Germany, until we were asked to leave.
No, there were only 30-some families, and there were no children my age that were Jewish.
No, it was not.
It was an industrial town.
It was built probably-- let me see-- possibly around the turn of the century.
The IG Farben, which was a factory, and the Bayer aspirin was discovered there.
And it became famous for that.
That is correct.
That's where they found the chemicals that produce the Bayer aspirin.
Today they produce many more things.
This was in the Rhineland.
We were on the Rhine River, and it was near the big city of Cologne-- not too far away.
My father's name was Leopold, L-E-O-P-O-L-D Heumann.
I think it was 1877, something like that.
From what I heard about him-- he died when I was just over a year-and-a-half old.
I heard that he was a very active man.
He was a father of four sons, and I was the change-of-life baby.
The-- let's see-- Mother was about 45 when I was born, and Dad was about 53 at that time.
And I was a surprise.
And Mother made a big fuss over my birth.
She said she always wanted a girl.
Dad seemed to be very proud of me.
The only way I know that is I have one picture of him holding me, and his face is beaming.
And I played with all his athletic memorabilia that were in the house-- the walking sticks with many insignias on it, and which walks he won awards for.
He loved to walk in the woods.
He was active civically.
He was in the town council.
And he came there to Leverkusen as a young married man with my mother, as a pioneer.
He did all the contracting for Bayer.
He had about 100 men working with him to decorate the plant, as well as the many homes that the Bayer housed their people in.
He was a businessman.
When that project with Bayer was finished, he founded his own business, which was called Heumann und Sohne, Heumann and Sons.
It was my parents' plan to have a family business.
Mother always worked with him.
When dad died, she took over the business.
And my two brothers-- the oldest one took over the contracting end of it, Alfred, and Helmut did the accounting.
And Joe was still working as an apprentice, learning the business and the contracting.
Yes, he was called in for World War I.
He served, which was quite a hardship for Mother.
That was a terrible war for all the participants, as we know today.
He served in France, where he was wounded.
He was in the army as a paramedic, and Mother talked about his bravery.
And he did get the highest German award, the Cross of Iron, for his bravery during that war.
And he came back not ever the same.
He was severely wounded.
He had shrapnel in his legs that couldn't be removed.
It kind of traveled around.
And he still had it when he died.
From what I hear, yes, they did.
They were partners, my parents.
That was a very traditional family.
Father was the typical German male head of the household who took on his responsibility with a lot of seriousness.
And he was a stern father.
Selma, S-E-L-M-A, Rosenberg.
Her middle name was Sarah.
Selma Sarah Rosenberg.
That was her birth name.
My mother was-- the people call her a saint, whoever knew her.
She was a real lady, a gentle woman, very courageous.
She had her fears.
She also had sort of a fatalism about the Jews.
She kind of sensed that someday the things that took place in the history of the Jewish people would again happen.
It was kind of a given.
She was very conscious of what they call-- they had an expression in Germany called [GERMAN], which meant prejudice against the Jews.
And she was concerned about causing any ripples in the community.
We had to be good citizens.
We had to behave properly.
And she was proud of all of us.
We did when she asked.
And of course, I was a little girl.
I lived in Germany until I left at age 11.
One-and-a-half.
Yes, she did.
She said that having me, having a baby, made her very happy.
It kept her from crying too much, because she didn't want me to see her cry.
It gave her some courage, and she worked very hard in the store.
She hired good people that took care of me in the household.
We always had pets.
We had a dog.
We had a series of dogs called Boobe-- and they would get killed.
They'd run in the street, and they'd get killed.
And it was always very traumatic, but she couldn't control them.
We lived on a business street.
We lived in the buildings that my father built when he was a pioneer in the town.
There were two apartment buildings side by side.

The business was in one of the buildings on the main floor, and it extended to the back, where he had a storehouse for all his paint and equipment, and where he stored the wallpaper and the rugs that we sold in the-- it was called a paint store.
And he ran contracting out of that as well.
Above the store was a first-floor, big apartment which was quite modern for the time.
We had a bathroom.
We had-- it was nicely furnished.
My father collected antiques, and we had his antiques in there, which traveled to this country later on.
Now, across the way, we had a roof garden on the first floor, so I was able to play in the roof garden.
Beyond the roof garden was a gate which led to a crosswalk to another building, and that building was a two-story building that housed his paint storage place underneath.
And on top there were two rooms.
One was for storage, and one was a comfortable room for the maid, where she stayed.
She had a bedroom.
They were built by my father.
From that second building, there was a long stairs going downstairs.
And then there was a back yard that encompassed both those apartment buildings.
A courtyard.
Very pretty.
It was landscaped, and it was totally hidden from the street.
We were very close.
I liked her meaningful eyes and how she looked at me with so much pride.
She said that having had me kept her young, and I believe it, because she was an active mother.
And my memory when I was growing up as a preteen and a teen, my girlfriends used to think my mother was so old, and they felt sorry for me.
I would hear this through the grapevine.
They never said it to me directly.
But I never looked at her as old.
She was my only mama.
Yes.
There was a huge age difference, and it did impact on me when I was a teen in America, and of course, she didn't understand me any longer when I became American.
Because once I came to this country, I didn't want any part of being German anymore.
It was during the war, and I really grew away from things German at that time.
That's correct.
Alfred was 22 years when I was born, and he was in Stuttgart where he was taking art courses.
He was a magnificently talented artist.
And of course, he knew the contracting in the store already, but he wanted to get away from home a little bit and study on his own.
And when he heard that my mother was pregnant, he couldn't believe it.
In fact, he was embarrassed, because when he came back to town, he thought that people would think-- and they did-- that I was his child, because he took me for long walks along the Rhine River.
And I have pictures of that, and they're very precious to me.
The older brother and the younger sister.
And I was all dressed up on Sundays, with a little hat on, a little coat, and we would go hand-in-hand and walk on the Rhine River.
And yes, he had to explain that this was his little sister.
He was very much like my father, in that he was the oldest in the family, and in Germany people had specific roles that they followed.
And the oldest had the most responsibility.
So he was the leader of the gang.
And I was very close to him all my life.
We also went through some hard times in this country, when I had to live in his home and robbed him of some of his privacy.
But he was there for me always when I needed him, and he really saved our lives.
He's the one that was responsible that we finally got out.
What else can I talk about Alfred?
Lots of things, but I don't know how much time you want to devote to him.
Who was your next brother?
The next brother was Helmut.
Helmut and Alfred looked quite different.
Alfred was called [GERMAN] because he was very tall.
Helmut was shorter.
Helmut had big, dark eyes and dark hair, and he was very smoochy and affectionate.
He used to say, [GERMAN], which means 'a big hug.' And he hugged me a lot, and he was a dear, dear brother also.
And he was the accountant in the family.
He was less artistic than the others, but he was very businesslike and very meticulous.
next brother was Werner.
And I always looked up to Werner as an athlete, because when he left our home, I played with all his box-fulls of laurel leaves that he won for high jumping.
All my brothers were very different.
He had wavy hair.
His face looked just like my mama.
A very handsome man-- to this day.
He's in his 80s, and he's still very handsome, a very fine person.
And he was a mechanical engineer.
He was the only one that wasn't in the family business.
And then there was Joe.
Joe was the closest in age to me.
He was 15 when I was born.
And my memories-- my early memories of Joe are he rough-housed with me.
He took me for sleigh rides in the winter.
In the summer we swim in the Rhine River.
And of course, he didn't know how to swim.
I didn't know this.
I found it out when I was older.
But he tried to dunk me in the Rhine River until I couldn't hold my breath anymore, and I was so angry with him.
And it wasn't until later I found out he couldn't even swim.
But he was very dear to me.
And Joe was also not a tall person.
He was on the short side, but he had a tremendous sense of humor.
He was just full of life.
Yes, I did.
They also spoiled me.
I was spoiled rotten.
Grandma was very, very old, as I recall her.
Now, she was my mother's stepmother.
My mother lost her mother when she was a young girl, and her father-- my grandfather-- married his widowed sister-in-law.
So that was really my mom's aunt that he married, and they did have a good relationship.
Now, she and my grandfather had one child together, Martha.
We pronounced it 'Marta,' Tante Marta.
And she was my closest aunt.
She would read to me in the bad times in Germany.
She came to live with us when we no longer lived in our home, but when we were forced to move into that maid's quarters that I described before, we shared that with her.
Three people lived in that room.
Very makeshift, but my mother made the best out of that.
And I recall the stories that Aunt Martha told me.
And when we were about to leave the country, she did many beautiful things for me.
She started me on getting memorabilia, such as an autograph book, where I collected autographs and messages from all the people that I left behind in Germany.
I have that book today.
It's just a small, little book, but it's very poetic, very poetic, the things people said to me.
Both my close family, my friends, my teachers wrote in there.
Both the teacher in the German public school and in the private Jewish school wrote to me in that little book.
And Aunt Martha herself wrote a beautiful, long message in there to me to remember her.
And even my mama wrote in the book, and she said, always remember your father.
And of course, I don't really remember him, having been so young.
But she did her best to tell me that I had a loving father.
And the other book that Aunt Martha gave me is a book of all the pictures that she could find of me growing up from the time I was the baby, including the one of my dad holding me.
Pictures of my little boyfriends that I had, pictures of my birthday parties, pictures of my visits to my other dear aunt, my mother's natural sister.
That was Tante Tilde, who married Jakob Daniels, and he was a farmer.
I loved visiting with Tante Tilde and Uncle Jakob in this town called Drove, a very tiny, little farm town.
And again, they were very sparse-- few Jews in that town.
No, they did not survive, none of them.
No.
We know what happened to her.
When we left, she moved back with Grandmother, and the two of them were taken away.
And there was another old lady that lived with them.
And the three of them were taken to Theriesenstadt, and Aunt Martha, I had heard later, beat the Nazis to killing her by dying from pneumonia.
I'd love to.
Mother kept Passover.
We changed dishes.
We had magnificent ceramic dishes that she would take out once a year, and I would love to help her with all the preparations.
And we would put matzohs and break them into a cup, pour hot coffee over them, and eat it like cereal.
It was delicious.
And I would help her peel all the vegetables and make all the preparations for all the holidays.
She was religious in the sense that she kept each holiday as a holy day, and we would walk to the synagogue.
It must've taken about an hour-and-a-half.
It was in the town of Opladen.
O-P-L-A-D-E-N, which is now part of Leverkusen.
Leverkusen's now divided into these different little towns.
They were all incorporated, and it's now one big city.
But we walked to Opladen, just she and myself, and we would attend this tiny little synagogue in Opladen.
It was a brick building.
I just remember it as a little square building.
It had a [GERMAN] on top, and that's how you knew from the outside that it was a synagogue.
It was tiny, and it had two stories because the women sat upstairs, although this was a Jewish reform synagogue, the German type of reform.
And I remember the Hebrew prayers, and also German was spoken in there.
I don't remember much about context-- context of what was said in the synagogue.
But after we went to the synagogue for the services, she and I walked to the cemetery, which was also located in Opladen, and we visited my dad's grave.
And I have real clear collections from a very early time that his grave was located at the end of the cemetery.
You walked through an iron gate into the cemetery, and we walked straight down.
And on the right side, there was a big birch tree, with a big trunk, and you know, how the branches come down.
That was an intriguing, peaceful type of a tree, right next to his grave.
And we spent time there every holiday.
And I'm glad I had that recollection, because I had to identify that grave site later on when I went back to Germany.
And that was one of the strong reasons I went there, because I had no real love of going back to Germany.
I almost didn't go when they invited me back.
But one of our missions was to identify Dad's grave and re-erect a stone.
It had completely vanished when I came back.
There wasn't a stone.
I recall a-- a marble stone that was wide on his grave, with his name, of course, on there.
And that was destroyed totally.
The Nazis, during the war years.
The desecrated that whole cemetery, right.
I think later on we'll be able to talk about what I saw when I came back as an adult, about how thrilling it was that now there is a new headstone for him, as a result of our going back.
Passover was my favorite, and then Hanukkah.
Yes.
When I was small, I went to a private nursery school which was run by German nuns, but it was not-- there was no religious education connected to it.
They were very loving nuns.
I remember a picture in my album that Aunt Martha collected for me where I'm crying.
I'm always crying in the pictures, not because I was a sad girl, but in those days, they had these huge flash bulbs that went off, lights like a fire, and it scared me.
And I was crying in her arms.
And they put that in the newspaper, saying, the teacher from that school is comforting this little Jewish girl.
And-- but I went there quite peacefully.
There was no prejudice towards me shown in those years.
None, none-- but I had lots of little German girlfriends.
My favorite girlfriend was Erika Zuchs, and I asked for her when I came back, and I had found out that she committed suicide.
But I did speak with her sister.
I had a little friend named Gerd-- he came to my birthday parties.
He approached me when I came back, and he had saved a picture of our birthday party in my home where he and I are shown in the picture.
And I was friendly with him and many other little children.
I have school pictures, and I still remember a lot of their names.
And my girlfriends would call for me when-- when Hitler came and all those laws were passed already that Germans should not fraternize with Jews.
The children kept right on coming, and even the mayor's daughter.
And my mother, later on, when things got hotter and hotter, she told a little girl, Ruth is not here.
She went to Israel.
And she would hide me in the house, and she sent them on their way, because she didn't want their families to get in trouble for being seen with me.
That's correct.
I think I had friends up to about age seven or eight, and then I didn't have friends anymore, except for-- I can go into that in our next time that we talk.
OK, one little friend that I had was when we were forced to sell our buildings, when the law said we Jews could not own property anymore.
My mother had to choose from two people that were interested in the property as well as the business.
One of these people was our competitor.
They were a Catholic family.
And she sold to them because they were nice people.
And we were allowed us to stay in the maid's quarters.
And she also had the little boy play with me.
We became wonderful, close little companions.
He was a little younger than I was.
His name is Hans Egan.
He still lives in Germany.
And he's married now.
I saw him again as an adult, which was very thrilling.
We reverted back to our childhood days.
